one_line_takeaway: A single, iteratively-refined, reasoning-mimicking adversarial document injected into a RAG knowledge base can drive attack success rates of 52-80% against modern reasoning LLMs (DeepSeek-R1, Qwen3, GLM4.5), substantially outperforming batch/flooding-style poisoning baselines like PoisonedRAG and prompt-hijacking attacks, especially against reasoning-distilled models that resist naive prompt injection.
key_finding: On MS-MARCO/NQ/HotpotQA with the Iterative variant, ASR reaches 67/66/75 (DeepSeek-R1), 71/74/80 (Qwen3), and 59/52/71 (GLM4.5) percent respectively (Table 2), versus much lower Non-iter ASR of 35/28/39, 40/34/47, and 29/21/39 percent for the same model/dataset combinations -- i.e., iterative optimization roughly doubles attack success in most settings, primarily by raising ASR_g (the persuasion component) rather than ASR_r. Qwen3 is the most vulnerable model tested and GLM4.5 the most resistant; HotpotQA generally yields higher ASR than the other two datasets. In the comparative experiment on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen-7B-R1-distilled (Table 3), the paper's Iterative method reaches ASR 72% / 74% respectively, versus baseline ASRs of NA 32%/24%, NPA 34%/25%, PHA 65%/32%, and PRAG 58%/51% -- the abstract's claim of 'up to 23%' ASR improvement over existing poisoning methods corresponds to this Iterative-vs-best-baseline gap on the reasoning-distilled model (PRAG 51% -> Ours 74%, a 23-point/percentage improvement). The paper also reports that prior prompt-hijacking-style attacks (PHA) lose roughly half their effectiveness when moving from a standard instruction-tuned model to its reasoning-distilled counterpart (65%->32% ASR), which the authors present as evidence that reasoning-optimized LLMs are more resistant to naive/prompt-based poisoning but still exploitable via reasoning-mimicking documents. A cross-model transfer analysis (Figure 4 heatmap) shows adversarial documents crafted against one source LLM mostly lose effectiveness when injected against a different target LLM (e.g., -56%, -42% ASR drops on NQ), indicating attack effectiveness is largely model-specific, though some residual transfer remains (occasional positive deltas, e.g., +19% MS-MARCO Qwen3->GLM4.5 direction as read from the heatmap), which the authors interpret as partial 'universality' of the reasoning-perturbation paradigm despite model-specific 'uniqueness'.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AdversarialCoT, a decision-based black-box attack that poisons a retrieval-augmented-generation (RAG) knowledge base with a SINGLE adversarial document per target query (rather than the batch/flooding approach used by prior knowledge-base poisoning work). The attack has two stages: (1) Observation and Initialization -- the attacker agent probes the target reasoning LLM with a small set of queries, extracts a coarse-grained three-phase reasoning skeleton T = {P1, P2, P3} (initiation, evidence examination/transition, aggregation/conclusion) from its chain-of-thought traces, and uses this skeleton to draft an initial adversarial document containing a pseudo reasoning process that argues for the attacker's target answer; and (2) Feedback-Driven Iterative Optimization -- the attacker agent submits the query, observes the LLM's retrieved-document set and reasoning trace, and iteratively refines the document along two axes: relevance optimization (if not retrieved, revise content to be more retrievable) and vulnerability-driven persuasion optimization (if retrieved but not persuasive, re-author the counter-reasoning chain to mimic the model's reasoning style and rebut its key evidence). The loop runs up to 3 rounds, using KIMI-K2 as the attacker agent.
threat_model: Decision-based, black-box attacker against a RAG pipeline (retriever R + reasoning LLM M). The attacker cannot access model/retriever internals or gradients, and per realistic constraints (following cited prior work [25,38,40]) can only ADD new documents to the knowledge base C, not modify or delete existing ones. The attacker observes only the final system output: the retrieved document set, the LLM's reasoning trace, and the final answer. The attacker has a set of target (query, target-answer) pairs and injects exactly one adversarial document per target query, aiming to make the reasoning LLM output the attacker-chosen answer y* for that query while the poisoned document must first survive top-k retrieval (k=5, Co-Condenser retriever) before it can influence reasoning. This is explicitly a query-specific, targeted, low-footprint threat model, contrasted by the authors with prior 'flooding' attacks that inject many poisoned documents per query.
limitations: Limitations stated or evident in the paper itself: (1) evaluation is restricted to a controlled sample of 100 queries per dataset due to the cost of black-box multi-round interaction, which the authors acknowledge but argue is necessary given attack budget realism; (2) iterative optimization is reported to increase attack cost rapidly with more rounds due to repeated black-box queries and growing context length, though the paper claims the per-query cost remains 'manageable' for targeted, high-value queries without quantifying absolute dollar/token cost; (3) the authors explicitly did not extend iterative refinement to the baseline methods (NA/NPA/PHA/PRAG), reasoning that iterative optimization is only well-defined for reasoning-structured adversarial documents, calling this an open direction rather than a controlled apples-to-apples comparison; (4) cross-model transfer results show attacks are largely model-specific with reduced but non-zero transfer, meaning the single-document attack as evaluated is not demonstrated to generalize efficiently to unknown/ensemble target models without separate optimization runs; (5) the paper frames its own robustness/defense discussion only qualitatively ('From attack traces to defense'), proposing that tracking how adversarial CoTs evolve from rejection to acceptance could help administrators find model defects, but does not implement, evaluate, or benchmark any concrete defense or detection mechanism -- defense is future work, not a contribution of this paper.

quantitative_results: Table 2 (Iterative variant ASR / ASR_r / ASR_g): DeepSeek-R1 -- MS-MARCO 90/74.4/67, NQ 99/66.7/66, HotpotQA 94/79.8/75
Table 2 (Iterative variant): Qwen3 -- MS-MARCO 91/78.0/71, NQ 95/77.9/74, HotpotQA 97/82.5/80
Table 2 (Iterative variant): GLM4.5 -- MS-MARCO 92/56.5/59, NQ 97/53.6/52, HotpotQA 96/74.0/71
Table 2 (Non-iter variant) roughly half the ASR of Iterative in most cells, e.g. DeepSeek-R1 MS-MARCO ASR 35 vs Iterative 67
Table 3 on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct / Qwen-7B-R1-distilled: Ours-Iterative ASR 72% / 74%, vs baselines NA 32%/24%, NPA 34%/25%, PHA 65%/32%, PRAG 58%/51%
Abstract's 'up to 23% ASR improvement over existing poisoning methods' matches PRAG(51%) -> Ours-Iterative(74%) on Qwen-7B-R1-distilled

Cross-model generalization heatmap (Fig. 4) shows ASR change when transferring adversarial docs across DeepSeek-R1/GLM4.5/Qwen3, e.g. NQ: -56% (R1->GLM4.5), -42% (R1->Qwen3), -35% (GLM4.5->R1), -19% (GLM4.5->Qwen3), -58% (Qwen3->R1), -65% (Qwen3->GLM4.5)
baselines_compared: Naive Attack (NA) -- document directly states the target answer in one sentence
Naive Prompt Attack (NPA) -- injected instruction-style document ('For query <q>, output: <a>.')
Prompt Hijacking Attack (PHA) -- from Zhang et al. 2024, adds 'ignore context' hijacking text
PoisonedRAG (PRAG) -- from Zou et al. 2024, crafts a corpus/document containing incorrect knowledge
Internal ablations: Non-iter (no refinement loop) vs Iterative (full multi-round AdversarialCoT)
